attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents with a three-month history of painful joints affecting her wrists, ankles, and knees. She also complains of recurrent outbreaks of oral ulcers and a rash on her face. The patient denies taking any medications. On examination, her blood pressure is normal, and there is mild swelling of her left wrist and ankle. There is 1+ pitting oedema in her lower extremities bilaterally. Examination of the oral cavity reveals multiple painful ulcers on the tongue and buccal mucosa. The examination is otherwise unremarkable. Her laboratory studies show:

  • Haemoglobin: 122 g/L (115-155)

  • White blood cell count: 2.3 x 10^9/L (4.5-11)

  • Platelets: 82 x 10^9/L (150-400)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
In this case, the positive links supporting the diagnosis of SLE include the combination of joint pain, oral ulcers, facial rash, leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. These findings, along with the patient's demographic and clinical characteristics, raise the suspicion of SLE as the most likely diagnosis. It is important to consider that SLE is a complex autoimmune disease that can affect multiple organs and systems, and its clinical presentation can vary widely among individuals. SLE commonly presents with symptoms such as joint pain, fatigue, rash (typically a "butterfly" rash on the face), and systemic involvement.

Leukopenia, characterised by a low white blood cell count, and thrombocytopenia, indicated by a low platelet count, are laboratory findings that hold diagnostic significance in various conditions, including systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). In SLE, leukopenia can result from immune-mediated destruction of white blood cells or bone marrow suppression, potentially increasing the risk of infections and reflecting disease activity. Thrombocytopenia in SLE can arise from immune-mediated platelet destruction or impaired platelet production, leading to an elevated susceptibility to bleeding or bruising. The presence of leukopenia and thrombocytopenia, alongside the patient's clinical symptoms of joint pain, oral ulcers, and rash, support the diagnosis of SLE.

Herpes simplex can cause oral ulcers, commonly referred to as cold sores or fever blisters, which typically occur on or around the lips. These ulcers are usually painful and can be accompanied by flu-like symptoms, such as fever, headache, and muscle aches. In some cases, herpes simplex can also cause skin lesions or rashes. In the given case, the patient does have oral ulcers, but they are not limited to the lip area. The ulcers are described as being present on the tongue and buccal mucosa, which is not typical of herpes simplex infections.

Reactive arthritis typically follows an infection, usually gastrointestinal or genitourinary in nature. It is characterised by asymmetric joint involvement, particularly affecting the lower extremities. While reactive arthritis can cause joint pain, it does not usually cause oral ulcers or rash. In this case, the presence of oral ulcers, rash, and systemic symptoms make reactive arthritis less likely.

Gout is a form of arthritis caused by the deposition of uric acid crystals in joints, leading to sudden onset of severe joint pain, redness, swelling, and tenderness. The most commonly affected joint is the big toe. While gout can cause joint symptoms, it does not typically present with oral ulcers, rash, or systemic symptoms. Additionally, the patient's joint involvement in the wrists, ankles, and knees is not consistent with the typical pattern seen in gout.

Behçet's disease is a rare autoimmune condition characterised by recurrent oral and genital ulcers, skin lesions, eye involvement, and joint pain. It is an uncommon condition globally, but is most common along the ancient Silk Road in Turkey, Japan, Korea, and China. Although the presence of oral ulcers and joint pain overlap with Behçet's disease, the absence of genital ulcers and eye involvement, as well as the laboratory findings of leukopenia and thrombocytopenia, make SLE a more likely diagnosis than Behçet's disease.
learningPoints: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a complex autoimmune disease with multisystem involvement.

The general symptoms include: fever, malaise, arthralgias, myalgia, headache, and loss of appetite and weight. Nonspecific fatigue, fever, arthralgia, and weight changes are the most common symptoms in new cases or recurrent active SLE flares.
references: Justiz Vaillant AA, Goyal A, Varacallo M. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. [Updated 2023 Feb 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK535405/

Ameer MA, Chaudhry H, Mushtaq J, Khan OS, Babar M, Hashim T, Zeb S, Tariq MA, Patlolla SR, Ali J, Hashim SN, Hashim S. An Overview of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) Pathogenesis, Classification, and Management. Cureus. 2022 Oct 15;14(10):e30330. doi: 10.7759/cureus.30330. PMID: 36407159; PMCID: PMC9662848.
